Journal.Title: Journal of the American College of Surgeons

Article.ArticleTitle: Functional Status after Ventral Hernia Repair: Longitudinal Trends and the Limits of Improvement.

Abstract.AbstractText: The impact of ventral hernias on physical function remains unclear. This study evaluates the association between hernia repair and patient functional status over time.

As part of the Pilot Trial of Abdominal Core Rehabilitation To Improve Outcomes After Ventral Hernia Repair (NCT05142618; 12/02/2021), we analyzed prospective data between 4/2022-8/2024. Patients completed Timed Up & Go (TUG) and Five Times Sit-to-Stand (5xSTS) tests preoperatively and at 4 weeks, 10 weeks, 6 months, and 1 year postoperatively. Generalized Estimating Equations modeling and Quadratic Trend analyses assessed changes in functional test scores over time.

Among 127 participants (mean age 51 years, mean BMI 33.07 kg/m2, 61% male, mean hernia width 7.02cm [SD 6.91cm]), pre-operative mean TUG was 8.48s (SD 2.22s) and 5xSTS was 14.34s (SD 5.25s). Functional test scores worsened at 4 weeks post-surgery (TUG estimate 0.94 [SE 0.35], p=0.007; 5xSTS estimate 1.81 [SE 0.73], p=0.014). Thereafter, only 5xSTS demonstrated significant improvement at 10 weeks (estimate -1.13 [SE 0.35], p=0.001), 6 months (estimate -1.32 [SE 0.34], p<0.001), and 1 year (estimate -1.50 [SE 0.31], p<0.001). 5xSTS's rate of improvement slowed over time, indicating a plateau effect (linear term estimate -0.121 [0.04], p=0.002; quadratic term estimate 0.002 [SE 0.0007], p=0.045).

Physical function testing provides a practical means to assess longitudinal functional status after ventral hernia repair. In this study, 5xSTS performance had early decline, progressive recovery, and plateau by 1 year after surgery. TUG showed little long-term change. These findings establish 5xSTS as a valuable tool for tracking functional status trajectories and highlight the need to further evaluate rehabilitation strategies to optimize patient outcomes.
